coredata.dc:title: Hybrid ultrafiltration membranes based on PES and MOFs @ carbon quantum dots for improving anti-fouling performance
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Han
Liu, Jun
Mu, Yongfeng
Lu, Nan
Zhang, Shuling
Zhang, Mei
Luan, Jiashuang
Wang, Guibin
coredata.dc:description: Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have raised considerable interest because of their favorable impacts on the performance of water treatment membranes. Herein, the novel nanocomposite based on both nanoporous MOFs and hydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was exploited as a novel dopant for the fabrication of PES ultrafiltration (UF) membranes. CQDs were synthesized via pyrolysis of citric acid, and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs were synthesized via the condensation reactions of CQDs and UiO-66-NH2 using EDC and NHS as the condensating agents. The properties of resulting membranes on the porosity, membrane morphology, hydrophilicity, permeability and anti-fouling performance were adequately investigated. The membranes incorporated with UiO-66-NH2@CQDs exhibited significantly improved water flux (up to 358.5 Lm−2h−1), which was ~1.82 times that of pristine PES membrane. Additionally, the BSA rejections rates for the hybrid membranes were all maintained above 95.0%. Furthermore, the hybrid membranes exhibited enhanced anti-fouling performance and the highest flux recovery ratio (FRR) of the hybrid membrane with 0.5% UiO-66-NH2@CQDs reached 77.6%. The membranes incorporated with UiO-66-NH2@CQDs exhibited better permeability and anti-fouling performance than those incorporated with CQDs or UiO-66-NH2. This work reveals that the novel hydrophilic nanofillers combining CQDs and MOFs exhibit great potential for application in water treatment membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrafiltration membranes
Metal organic frameworks
Carbon quantum dots
Filtration efficiency
Antifouling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Overall porosity and mean pore radius
Characterization of UiO-66-NH2, CQDs and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs
Overall porosity and mean pore radius of membrane

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are really grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774052), 2018 Talent Development Fund Centralized Funding Project, Jilin Province and Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Project of China (20200801003GH).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the fabrication process for the hybrid membranes.
(a) XRD patterns of UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs, (b) FTIR spectra, (c) TGA curves, (d) XPS spectra, of CQDs, UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs.
TEM images of (a) UiO-66-NH2, (b) CQDs, and (c) UiO-66-NH2@CQDs.
The cross-section and top surface SEM images of (a) M−0, (b) M−C, (c) M−M, (d) M−1, (e) M−2, (f) M−3, and (g) M−4.
Water contact angles of the membranes.
(a) Water fluxes and rejections, (b) flux recovery ratio, (c) time-dependent water permeation fluxes, (d) summary of Rt , Rr , and Rir of membranes.
XPS elemental analyses of the surfaces of nanoparticles.
Porosity and mean pore size data for the membranes.

head.ce:title: Hybrid ultrafiltration membranes based on PES and MOFs @ carbon quantum dots for improving anti-fouling performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of High Performance Plastics, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High Performance Plastics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of High Performance Plastics, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Hand Surgery, The Second Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130041, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Hand Surgery, The Second Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130041, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhuhai College, Jilin University, Zhuhai 519041, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhuhai College, Jilin University, Zhuhai 519041, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have raised considerable interest because of their favorable impacts on the performance of water treatment membranes. Herein, the novel nanocomposite based on both nanoporous MOFs and hydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was exploited as a novel dopant for the fabrication of PES ultrafiltration (UF) membranes. CQDs were synthesized via pyrolysis of citric acid, and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs were synthesized via the condensation reactions of CQDs and UiO-66-NH2 using EDC and NHS as the condensating agents. The properties of resulting membranes on the porosity, membrane morphology, hydrophilicity, permeability and anti-fouling performance were adequately investigated. The membranes incorporated with UiO-66-NH2@CQDs exhibited significantly improved water flux (up to 358.5 Lm−2h−1), which was ~1.82 times that of pristine PES membrane. Additionally, the BSA rejections rates for the hybrid membranes were all maintained above 95.0%. Furthermore, the hybrid membranes exhibited enhanced anti-fouling performance and the highest flux recovery ratio (FRR) of the hybrid membrane with 0.5% UiO-66-NH2@CQDs reached 77.6%. The membranes incorporated with UiO-66-NH2@CQDs exhibited better permeability and anti-fouling performance than those incorporated with CQDs or UiO-66-NH2. This work reveals that the novel hydrophilic nanofillers combining CQDs and MOFs exhibit great potential for application in water treatment membranes.

ce:section.ce:para: As global industrialization continues to expand, water contamination has become a severe societal problem and solutions are urgently needed [1]. Membrane separation techniques exhibit promise as safe, effective, and facile methods for wastewater treatment, and they can readily be applied in water-recycling systems. If appropriate membrane separation techniques are used, impurities in sewage can be removed, and, therefore, membrane separation techniques play an important role in social development and environmental protection. Membrane separation is considered to be economically feasible, and it provides several advantages, including good selectivity, high energy efficiency, and low chemical utilization. Consequently, ultrafiltration (UF) has been extensively used for wastewater treatment, food production, biological separations, and other fields, and it can effectively remove colloids and macromolecules [2,3]. UF membranes show immense potential for wastewater purification, and the selective permeabilities of membranes could protect water resources from natural organic matter [4,5].

However, UF membranes also suffer from austere problems such as low water filtration flux and membrane fouling. Membrane fouling decreases the permeation flux of UF membranes, and it severely compromises the separation performance as well [6]. Therefore, modifications are required to reduce the degree of fouling and improve the service lives of membranes. Currently, there are several methods employed for modification of UF membranes, among those physical blending has proved to be an excellent and convenient method. The modification of physical blending on UF membranes, involves simple preparation methods for the introduction of hydrophilic nanomaterials, and they can enhance the hydrophilicity and anti-fouling performance of membranes [7]. Nanoparticles have already been employed into UF membranes, which include carbon nanotubes [8], TiO2 nanoparticles [9] , halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) [10,11], graphene oxide (GO) [12], hydroxyapatite nanotubes (HANTs) [13], and metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [14] . Liu et al. claimed that sulfonated HNTs are effective dopants for the preparation of ultrafiltration membranes. When 8 wt% sulfonated hyperbranched polyethersulfone-modified halloysite nanotubes (HNT-SHBPES) were mixed into the casting solution, the water flux of the resulting membrane reached 351.6 L/m2h, a level ~2.2 times that of PES membranes [15]. Liu et al. first grafted perfluorosulfonic acid (PFSA) moieties onto GO, and the water flux of the membrane incorporated with PFSA-g-GO was ~1.5 times that of PVDF membrane [16]. Mu et al. fabricated nanocomposite UF membranes consisting of modified HANTs (HANTs-DA-TA), and the hybrid membranes with 0.3 wt% HANTs-DA-TA exhibited remarkable water fluxes that were ~2.6 times that of PES membranes [17].

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline materials with periodic network structures, typically organized by self-assembly of transition metals (metal ions or metal clusters) and organic ligands. As novel nanomaterials, MOFs possess the benefits of high porosity, big specific surface area, regular channels, adjustable pore diameter, etc. Numerous metal organic frameworks materials have already been synthesized, and they typically include carboxyl-containing organic anion ligands or neutral nitrogen-containing heterocyclic ligands [18–21]; examples include IRMOFs [22], ZIFs [23], and MILs [24]. The amine-functionalized UiO-66 (UiO-66-NH2) is the derivative of UiO-66, on which the surface contains amino and a few carboxyl groups [25,26]. Consequently, UiO-66-NH2 is selected for the current study. UiO-66-NH2 can be synthesized with ZrCl4 and 2-aminoterephthalic acid via a solvothermal method [27]. However, the hydrophilic groups of MOFs surface are not abundant, so the functional groups on the surface are used to modify by incorporating the nanoparticles with numerous functional groups to improve hydrophilicity and compatibility with the polymer matrix.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a carbon-based material. CQDs exhibit numerous advantages such as excellent optical properties, outstanding water solubility, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness. CQDs are also widely applied in medical imaging technology, chemical analysis, catalyst preparation and other fields [28–31]. Over the past decade or so, scientists had developed all kinds of methods for the synthesis of CQDs; these could be roughly divided into top-down and bottom-up methods depending on the carbon source. Top-down syntheses are physical or chemical methods for peeling small quantum dots from large carbon sources. The carbon sources of CQDs synthesized by top-down methods are generally carbon nanotubes, graphite rods, and activated carbon, etc. These carbon-rich substances are decomposed by means of arc discharge, laser erosion, electrochemical synthesis, and other methods, and they ultimately generate quantum dots. Bottom-up methods, in contrast, employ very small carbon materials to synthesize quantum dots. CQDs synthesized using bottom-up methods often employ small organic molecules or oligomers as carbon sources, including citric acid, glucose, and carbamide. Typical bottom-up methods utilize chemical oxidation, hydrothermal/solvent-thermal methods, template methods, etc [32–35]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), containing numerous oxygen-containing groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl groups), are effective nanofillers of the hydrophilic material, but their strong hydrophilicity and aggregation hinder their application. The application of porous media such as MOFs may constitute an effective method and effectively improve aggregation of CQDs to obtain ideal nanomaterials, and the synergistic effects of CQDs and MOFs may provide to fabricate high-performance UF membranes.

In this study, UiO-66-NH2@CQDs, combining the nanoporous structures of MOFs and the hydrophilicity of CQDs, were successfully synthesized, and, to our knowledge, this research constitutes the first application of this nanomaterial in the field of ultrafiltration membranes. Thus, a series of PES nanocomposite membranes were prepared using the nonsolvent induced phase separation technique by incorporating different quantities of the nanoparticles. Chemical structures and the compositions of nanoparticles were determined with the use of Fourier-Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In addition, the morphologies of the membranes were observed with scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The hydrophilicity and ultrafiltration performance of the hybrid membranes were completely characterized

Polyethersulfone (PES, MWCO = 62,000–64,000 g/mol) was purchased from Solvay Chemical. 2-aminoterephthalic acid (C8H7NO4, >98.0%), citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥99.5%), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, C3H7NO, >99.5%), methanol (CH4O, >99.5%), formic acid (CH2O2, >99.0%), N-methylpyrrolidinone (NMP, >99.0%), Na2HPO4·12H2O, and NaH2PO4·2H2O were all purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China. Zirconium (IV) chloride (ZrCl4, >98%) was purchased from Energy Chemical, China. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, K30, MWCO = 30000 g/mol) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, MO USA. Bovine serum albumin (BSA, MW66kDa, >99.0%) was purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China. N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98%) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%) were all purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China as the condensating agent.

UiO-66-NH2 was synthesized via a process similar to that reported by Cao et al [27]. Typically, ZrCl4 (0.233 g) and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (0.363 g) were dissolved in DMF (50 mL). Subsequently, formic acid (13.5 mL) was added in dropwise fashion to this solution with stirring. The mixed solution was then transferred into a stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the mixture was centrifuged, and the precipitates were washed several times with DMF and methanol respectively. Finally, the UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles were soaked in methanol for three days and then desiccated at 60 °C in a vacuum for 18 h.

A pyrolysis method was used to prepare CQDs [36]. First, citric acid powder was ground and added to a stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave, and then heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the resulting production was dissolved in pure water and then dialyzed with a dialysis bag (1 kDa) until there was no further variation in the conductivity of the ambient water. The solution was then freeze dried to remove water. CQDs with carboxyl groups were obtained.

Ground on the above two steps, UiO-66-NH2@CQDs was synthesized via condensation reaction. CQDs (0.40 g) were dissolved in pure water (50 mL), and NHS (2.302 g) and EDC (3.834 g) were added to the CQDs solution with stirring. Subsequently, UiO-66-NH2 powder (0.20 g) were dispersed in pure water (10 mL) and sonicated for 0.5 h. The UiO-66-NH2 suspension was then added to the EDC/NHS mixed solution in dropwise fashion with stirring. The resulting precipitates were collected by centrifugation and washed five times with pure water, then desiccated at 60 ℃ under vacuum for 24 h to obtain the UiO-66-NH2@CQDs nanoparticles.

The pristine PES membrane and hybrid PES membranes were manufactured via a phase inversion process by using PVP as pore-forming agent [17,37]. The detailed compositions of the casting solutions are listed in Table1 .

Initially, a weighed sample of nanoparticles was dispersed in NMP solvent and the suspension was sonicated for 30 min. Appropriate weights of PES and PVP were added, and, the solutions were then stirred for 8 h at ambient temperature. After the casting solutions were fully outgassed, they were cast onto the clean glass plates using a cast knife with 150 μm gap and subsequently immerged in coagulation baths (pure water) for 30 min after 30 s of exposure to air. The membranes were transferred to a new coagulation bath and repeated three times to remove remaining PVP and NMP. The prepared membranes were marked as M−0, M−C, M−M, M−1, M−2, M−3, or M−4. Schematic diagram of the whole experiment process was presented in Fig. 1 .

The crystalline property of the MOFs was determined with XRD spectra. The XRD patterns for MOFs and modified MOFs nanoparticles were obtained with an X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean, PANalytical B.V.). The Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained with a Nicolet iS10 Fourier-Transform Infrared spectrophotometer in the range 400 to 4000 cm−1. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed with a NETZSCH STA499F3 QMS403D instrument, using a temperature range of 40 to 800 ℃ and a heating rate of 10 ℃/min under an air atmosphere. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer. The synthesized UiO-66-NH2, CQDs and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs were characterized with a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-1200EX, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overall porosity and mean pore radius
ce:section.ce:para:
The porosities of the membranes were obtained by measuring the differences in weights of wet membranes and dried membranes. The overall porosity (ε) was defined with the following equation:
(1)
ε =
W w
-
W d
A × l × ρ
× 100 %
where ε denotes the porosity of the membrane, Ww is the weight of the wet membrane, Wd is the weight of the dry membrane. A is the area of the membrane (cm2), l is the thickness of membrane (cm), and ρ denotes the density of the pure water (g cm−3).

The mean pore radius (rm ) of the membrane was calculated with the Guerout–Elford–Ferry formula [38]:
(2)
r m
=
2.9 - 1.75 ε
× 8 η l Q
ε × A × Δ P
where rm represents the mean pore radius (cm), η denotes the water viscosity (8.937 × 10-4N s m−2), Q stands for the volume of pure water permeating per unit time (m3 s−1) and ΔP represents the operating pressure (0.1 MPa).
To compare the morphologies of pristine membrane and the hybrid membranes, images were photographed with a FEI Nova NanoSEM 450 microscope field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM).
The hydrophilicity of membrane surfaces was measured with a contact angle analyzer (Drop Shape Analyzer, DSA-25). Each sample was measured three times and recorded, and the mean value was calculated.

The filtration experiments were performed to assess the UF performance of the pristine membrane and hybrid membranes with cross flow filtration equipment, using an effective membrane area of 7.1 cm2. The membranes were compacted with pure water at 0.15 MPa for 0.5 h. Subsequently, the operating pressure was switched to apply the test pressure (0.1 MPa). The flux values of membranes were recorded every 5 min for 1 h at ambient temperature. The initial pure water flux (Jw,1 ) was determined according to the follow formula:
(3)
J =
V
A × T
where J represents the flux (L m−2h−1), V is the filtration volume of the permeated solution (L), A denotes the valid area of membrane (m2), and T stands for the permeation time (h).

Afterwards, the feed solution was changed to a BSA solution (1 g/L, pH = 7.4, Na2HPO4·12H2O and NaH2PO4·2H2O as the phosphate buffer solution), and the pressure was held constant for 2 h. After filtering the BSA solution, the membranes were cleaned with pure water, and the flux was measured again with pure water. Rejection (R) and flux recovery ratio (FRR) were determined with Eqs. (4) and (5):
(4)
R
%
=
1 -
C p
C f
× 100
(5)
F R R
%
=
J
w , 2
J
w , 1
× 100

Here it should be noted that Cf and Cp are the BSA concentrations of the feed solution and permeation, respectively, and Jw,2 , and Jw,1 represent the water fluxes of polluted membranes and initial membranes, respectively.

Total fouling ratio (Rt ), reversible fouling ratio (Rr ), and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir ) were calculated with Eqs. (6)–(8):
(6)
R t
%
=
1 -
J P
J
w , 1
× 100
(7)
R
ir
%
=
1 -
J
w , 2
J
w , 1
× 100
(8)
R r
=
R t
-
R
ir
Here, Jp is the flux for the BSA solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of UiO-66-NH2, CQDs and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate further the crystalline structures of the MOFs, nanoparticles were analyzed by XRD. Fig. 2
a displayed the XRD spectra of the UiO-66-NH2 and the UiO-66-NH2@CQDs. The XRD patterns were almost identical to the simulated pattern reported for UiO-66 in the literature [39]. After the CQDs were grafted, the positions of the diffraction peaks for UiO-66-NH2@CQDs were in conformity to UiO-66-NH2, which indicated that the crystalline structure was not destroyed, and the framework of MOFs was well maintained.

The FT-IR data further confirmed the chemical composition of the UiO-66-NH2@CQDs. The FT-IR spectra of UiO-66-NH2, CQDs, and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs were displayed in Fig. 2 b. The characteristic bands at 3358, 3344, and 3300 cm−1 were attributed to the OH stretching vibrations for hydroxyl groups [27,40]. For UiO-66-NH2, a characteristic peak observed at 3466 cm−1 was attributed to the NH stretching vibration of 2-aminoterephthalic acid [39]. Absorption peaks at 1710 cm−1 appeared in the spectra. They were due to the CO stretching vibration for the CQDs (carboxyl) and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs (carboxyl and amide bonds) [40], indicating that the CQDs were successfully immobilized in the MOFs. A peak at 1402 cm−1, an absorption peak observed for three types of nanoparticles, was attributed to a CH stretching vibration [41]. In addition, characteristic peaks for the amino groups could be discerned, which included the NH bending vibration at 1577 cm−1 and CN stretching vibrations at 1233 cm−1 (UiO-66-NH2@CQDs) and 1265 cm−1 (UiO-66-NH2), attributed to aromatic amines [39]. These spectral characteristics demonstrated the successful synthesis of UiO-66-NH2@CQDs.

TGA was also employed to verify the successful synthesis of UiO-66-NH2@CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 c, the MOFs exhibited higher thermal stability and lost 63.77% of weight eventually. For UiO-66-NH2, two main weight loss steps took place at approximately 170–500 ℃ and 625–700 ℃ respectively. The TGA data for CQDs exhibited major weight changes at approximately 135–310 ℃ and 450–595 ℃, and there was a weight loss of 99.1%. After grafting the CQDs, the TGA curve of the modified MOFs exhibited two weight loss steps (335–500 ℃ and 625–700 ℃), which correspond to UiO-66-NH2; two other endothermic peaks corresponded to moisture evaporation (50–125 ℃) and the presence of CQDs (135–300 ℃). Hence, the TGA curves further confirmed that CQDs were immobilized on the MOFs.

XPS spectra for the nanoparticles and XPS elemental analyses of the surfaces of nanoparticles were displayed in Fig. 2 d and Table 2 , respectively. As could be seen from Fig. 2 d, the UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs nanoparticles exhibited peaks for C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and Zr 3d [25], while the CQDs have peaks for C 1 s and O 1 s [42]. Table 2 revealed that elements Zr and N contents of UiO-66-NH2@CQDs were less than those of UiO-66-NH2, because the grafting of CQDs led to a decrease in the ratio of hetero atoms in the MOFs.

TEM images for UiO-66-NH2, CQDs, and UiO-66-NH2@CQDs were displayed in Fig. 3 , and the morphologies of the nanoparticles was observed by TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, UiO-66-NH2 exhibited a typical octahedral structure and the particle sizes were typically smaller than 500 nm. Fig. 3 b shows that the CQDs had a nearly spherical structure with particles ranging in size from 4.3 to 12.5 nm and average size of 7.95 nm (the data were displayed in Fig. S1 ). Fig. 3 c displayed the TEM image for UiO-66-NH2@CQDs. There were a number of CQDs observed around the edges of the UiO-66-NH2, which directly verified that CQDs were successfully grafted to UiO-66-NH2 [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overall porosity and mean pore radius of membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The porosity and mean pore radius are significant factors affecting membrane permeability, and these factors play a crucial role for water fluxes and rejection performance of membranes. According to previous reports, the addition of an appropriate number of nanoparticles such as CNTs, HNTs, GO, and HANTs [17,44–46] could make for larger pore sizes and porosities, relative to those of the initial membrane, thus achieving better permeability. As shown in Table 3 , the membranes modified with hydrophilic nanoparticles exhibited higher pore sizes and porosities than did the pristine PES membrane. The membranes incorporated with UiO-66-NH2@CQDs had larger porosities and mean pore sizes than did the membranes incorporated with the same amounts of UiO-66-NH2 and CQDs. Furthermore, the porosities of the nanocomposite membranes became larger as the nanoparticle content was increased, until the content reached 0.3%, and then the porosity declined slightly. The introduction of the nanoparticles influenced the exchange rate between the solvent and nonsolvent phases during the phase inversion process, and increased the porosities and mean pore radiuses. However, the viscosity of the casting solution increased with increasing nanomaterial proportion and, when the viscosity increased to a certain level, the speed of the phase inversion slowed down; this resulted in lower porosity and mean pore radius.

Fig. 4 gave the SEM photographs of the surfaces and cross-sectional morphologies of membranes. A typical asymmetric structure was observed, with a compact top layer and a porous sublayer. It was found that the porous sublayer of the pristine PES membrane contained spongy holes and finger-like pores. By contrast, the membranes modified with nanoparticles exhibited the finger-like pores, and with the same content, the pores of the nanocomposite membranes were longer and broader than those of the membranes only incorporated with CQDs or UiO-66-NH2. Furthermore, the finger-like pores of the nanocomposite membranes became longer and broader with increasing of nanoparticle content until the content reached 0.3%, and then the pore sizes diminished slightly; this was consistent with the porosity data in Table 3. The transformation in the membrane morphology was in line with the change in porosity. There are two main factors that affect the morphologies of the membranes during phase inversion processes, namely thermodynamics and kinetics. It is thermodynamic factor that the introduction of hydrophilic nanoparticles, which reduced the thermodynamic stability of the system, and can facilitates the phase separation and augment the rate of exchange between solvent and nonsolvent during the phase inversion, thus generating larger and wider pores [25]. Nevertheless, as the nanoparticle content increase, the viscosity of casting solution, one of the kinetic factors, increased gradually. When the content of nanoparticles reaches 0.5%, the viscosity of the casting solution and aggregation of nanoparticles are the dominant factors influencing the phase inversion rate, so the expansion of the pores is suppressed [17].

Hydrophilicity exerts a significant impact on the performance of the membrane, and it can enhance the anti-fouling properties of the UF membrane. Generally, the membrane hydrophilicity is identified by estimating water contact angles of membranes. A lower value of contact angle corresponds to better hydrophilicity [27,47]. The results of water contact angle tests were shown in Fig. 5 . The membranes mixed with nanomaterials exhibited higher hydrophilicity than did the pristine PES membrane. Furthermore, the membrane containing 0.5% UiO-66-NH2@CQDs demonstrated the lowest contact angle of 59.8° resulting from the migration of nanoparticles to the surface of the membrane in the phase inversion. In addition, the porosity of the MOFs dispersed near the membranes surfaces facilitated the increase of water permeability, resulting in greater hydrophilicity of the membrane incorporated with UiO-66-NH2@CQDs relative to that incorporated with CQDs. However, the overall hydrophilicity of the membrane containing CQDs was better than that of the membrane containing UiO-66-NH2, since the CQDs contained more hydrophilic groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) than UiO-66-NH2 (with relatively few amino and carboxyl groups).

Fig. 6
a showed that, with the introduction of the nanoparticles, the water flux of the hybrid membrane was significantly increased and reached a maximum value (358.5 L/m2h) when the loading of UiO-66-NH2@CQDs was 0.3 wt%, corresponding to a 1.82-fold enhancement relative to that of pristine membrane (197.1 L/m2h). The water flux for the membrane incorporated with CQDs (273.0 L/m2h) was slightly greater than that of the membrane incorporated with UiO-66-NH2 (259.5 L/m2h) since the hydrophilic performance of the CQDs was better than that of UiO-66-NH2. Yet both of them were greater than that of the pristine membrane. With the same component ratios (0.3 wt%), the water filtration performance of the hybrid membrane was preferable than those of the membranes only incorporated with CQDs or UiO-66-NH2; this was because UiO-66-NH2@CQDs combined the hydrophilicity, dispersity, and compatibility of UiO-66-NH2 and CQDs. Nevertheless, when the UiO-66-NH2@CQDs particle content reached 0.5%, the increased viscosity of the casting solution and the aggregation of the nanoparticles led to a decrease in pore size and porosity, resulting in a reduction in water flux [17,48]. Moreover, the promotion of membrane flux was also related to the improved membrane hydrophilicity, as indicated by the contact angles tests [49]. Additionally, the rejection percentage for BSA remained at a high level with all membranes, which indicated the absence of defects in the surfaces of the membranes.

In practical applications, UF membranes would be polluted by contaminants in the environment, resulting in the degradation of ultrafiltration performance. In this study, BSA was employed in our experiments as a representative pollutant. After the pure water permeation test, membranes were contaminated by BSA; this was caused by the deposition of foulants within the valleys of membrane surface and blocking of membrane pores. Part of the lost water flux could be recovered after membrane cleaning. As depicted in Fig. 6, the FRR and Rr values of all hybrid membranes were greater than those of the pristine membrane since the incorporation of hydrophilic nanoparticles improved the hydrophilicity of hybrid membranes. The FRR and Rr values improved with the increasing nanoparticle content, and the FRR and Rr values of membranes incorporated with UiO-66-NH2@CQDs were greater than those of the membranes only incorporated with CQDs or UiO-66-NH2 at the same content. This indicated that the membranes incorporated with UiO-66-NH2@CQDs exhibited better anti-fouling performance than those of the membranes only incorporated with CQDs or UiO-66-NH2, because the membranes incorporated with UiO-66-NH2@CQDs were more hydrophilic than the membrane only incorporated with UiO-66-NH2 or CQDs (Fig. 5). Since the improvement of hydrophilicity of the hybrid membranes, the hydrated sheath could be organized on the surfaces of the membranes and prevented the adsorption of BSA, thus improving the resistance to fouling capability of hybrid membranes [50,51].

To assess the stability of the membranes, the pristine PES membrane and hybrid membranes were tested via circulating filtration. Fig. 6 c illustrated the variation of membranes fluxes made with different compositions over time. After 60 min of filtration with pure water, it could be observed that, after the filtrate was changed to a BSA solution, the fluxes of the membranes declined and attenuation lasted for 120 min due to the amassing and adsorption of BSA on the surfaces and in the pores of the membranes. Some of the BSA molecules in the membrane could not be removed, resulting in blockage of the membrane pores. After three cycles of ultrafiltration tests, it was observed that the hybrid membranes exhibited better stability and lower reduction in flux than the pristine PES membrane.

In this work, the UiO-66-NH2@CQDs was prepared and introduced into the ultrafiltration membranes for the first time, using a phase inversion technique. With the porosity of MOFs and the hydrophilicity of CQDs, UiO-66-NH2@CQDs exhibited better hydrophilicity and compatibility than UiO-66-NH2, and was less prone to aggregation than CQDs. Subsequently, the three kinds of nanoparticles were incorporated into the PES matrix to fabricate the novel hybrid UF membranes by means of the phase inversion method. The hybrid membranes exhibited superior permeability and anti-fouling properties than the pristine PES membrane. The membrane incorporated with 0.3% of UiO-66-NH2 exhibited an increased pure water flux of 358.5 L/m2h, which was ~ 1.82 times that of the pristine PES membrane. Moreover, the BSA rejection rate of the hybrid membranes was>95% and the maximum FRR was 77.6%. In summary, UiO-66-NH2@CQDs nanoparticles were shown to be a novel high-efficiency nanomaterial useful for preparing UF membranes. This work is a valuable reference for the fabrication of environmentally friendly nanofillers for UF membranes, and it demonstrates the significant prospects for the use of CQDs and UiO-66-NH2 in water treatment membranes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are really grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774052), 2018 Talent Development Fund Centralized Funding Project, Jilin Province and Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Project of China (20200801003GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.118586.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
